buggy_code:
import pandas as pd

# Two separate DataFrames for different time periods
df_2019 = pd.DataFrame({
    'price': [100, 101, 102],
    'volume': [1000, 1100, 1200]
}, index=pd.date_range('2019-12-30', periods=3))

df_2020 = pd.DataFrame({
    'price': [103, 104, 105],
    'volume': [1300, 1400, 1500]
}, index=pd.date_range('2020-01-02', periods=3))

# Concatenate but RESET the index
combined = pd.concat([df_2019, df_2020], ignore_index=True)

# Now compute returns
combined['returns'] = combined['price'].pct_change()

# Use as training features
X = combined[['volume']]
y = combined['returns']
clean_code:
import pandas as pd

df_2019 = pd.DataFrame({
    'price': [100, 101, 102],
    'volume': [1000, 1100, 1200]
}, index=pd.date_range('2019-12-30', periods=3))

df_2020 = pd.DataFrame({
    'price': [103, 104, 105],
    'volume': [1300, 1400, 1500]
}, index=pd.date_range('2020-01-02', periods=3))

# Preserve the date index
combined = pd.concat([df_2019, df_2020])

# Now pct_change respects time gaps correctly
combined['returns'] = combined['price'].pct_change()

# Drop the boundary return (cross-year transition)
combined = combined.dropna()
X = combined[['volume']]
y = combined['returns']
description: pd.concat with ignore_index=True resets the DatetimeIndex to integers 0, 1, 2...
explanation: ignore_index=True discards the date index and replaces it with integers. This causes pct_change() to compute the return between 2019-12-31 and 2020-01-02 as if they were consecutive days, ignoring the weekend/holiday gap. The return across the year boundary is computed as (103-102)/102 = 0.98% when the real return may be very different. All time-based operations (rolling, resample, shift) break silently because the index is now integers, not dates.
suggested_fix: Never use ignore_index=True when concatenating time series DataFrames. Preserve the DatetimeIndex so time-based operations remain valid.
